| name              = Eugene Rosenberg
| native_name       = יוג'ין רוזנברג
| native_name_lang  = he
| image             = EugeneR photo.jpg
| birth_date        = {{birth date and age|1935|10|16}}

| known_for         = [[Myxobacteria]], Microorganisms to Combat Pollution, [[hologenome]] concept
| education         = B.Sc. - [[UCLA]], Ph.D. - [[Columbia University]]
| field             = Microbiology, Biotechnology
| work_institution  = [[Tel Aviv University]]
| spouse            = Leah Petlak-Rosenberg (1938-1988), Ilana Zilber-Rosenberg (1942)
| children          = Robin Esther Doron, Stephanie Shosh Rotem, Denise Rosenberg
}}

'''Eugene Rosenberg''' ({{langx|he| יוג'ין רוזנברג}})  (October 16, 1935) is a microbiologist at the Faculty of Life Sciences at [[Tel Aviv University]],<ref name="member">{{Cite web| title =  The George S. Wise Faculty of Life Sciences, Tel Aviv University | url = https://en-lifesci.tau.ac.il/faculty_pages?id=177}}</ref> an expert in the field of applied [[environmental microbiology]],<ref name = asm>{{Cite web |title = Awardees of American Society for Microbiology|url = http://www.asm.org/index.php/component/content/article/114-unknown/unknown/4076-2003-general}}</ref> in particular his work on [[Myxobacteria]],<ref>Yang, Zhaomin and Penelope I. Higgs (eds.), ''Myxobacteria: Genomics, Cellular and Molecular Biology'' (Portland, OR: Caister Academic Press, 2014)</ref> microorganisms to combat pollution (bioremediation),<ref name="asm"/><ref name = "Times">{{Cite magazine |title = Oil Eaters |magazine = Time |volume=101 |issue=21 |pages=64|date = 1973-05-21}}</ref><ref name="awards">{{Cite web| title = Israeli scientist wins US award for ecological research| work= Israel21c Organization | date= 23 December 2002 | url = http://www.israel21c.org/israeli-scientist-wins-us-award-for-ecological-research/}}</ref><ref name = "bleaching2">{{Cite news|title = Saving corals with viruses?| work = Advanced Aquarist Publication | url = http://www.advancedaquarist.com/blog/saving-corals-with-viruses| last1 = Ho | first1 = Leonard }}</ref> and the [[Hologenome theory of evolution]] - described in his book.<ref name="OriginOfLifeBook"/>

==Early life and education==
Rosenberg was born in [[New York City]] in 1935, grew up in [[Los Angeles]], and immigrated to Israel in 1970.<ref name="awards"/> He received his Bachelor of Science from the [[University of California Los Angeles]] (UCLA) and his  Ph.D. from the Department of [[Biochemistry]] at [[Columbia University]], New York (1961).<ref name="asm"/> His doctoral thesis, under the supervision of Steven Zamenhof, describes the chemical structures of the capsules of [[Hemophilus influenzae]], types B, E, and F. Rosenberg went on to postdoctoral research in [[organic chemistry]] under the guidance of [[Lord Todd]] in [[Cambridge University]] (1962). Between 1962 and 1970 he was first Assistant and then associate professor of [[microbiology]] at UCLA,  concentrating on the biochemistry of [[Myxococcus xanthus]].
In 1970 he has been a member of the academic staff in the Department of [[Molecular Microbiology]] and [[Biotechnology]] at [[Tel Aviv University]], where he was appointed Full Professor in 1975 and [[Professor Emeritus]] in 2006. He has held the Gol Chair in Applied and Environmental Microbiology since 1989.<ref name="member"/>
Recently, he  served as the editor-in-chief for The Prokaryotes,<ref name = book_editor>{{Cite book |title = The Prokaryots, Editor-in-chief: Rosenberg Eugene (DeLong, E.F., Stackebrandt, E., Thompson, F., Lory, S. (Eds.)), 2013 | url = https://www.springer.com/us/book/9783642301438| isbn = 9783642301438| publisher = Springer| year = 2013| series = The Prokaryotes}}</ref> a comprehensive reference encyclopedia on Bacteria and Archea.

==Academic==
[[File:Eugene lab.jpg|thumb|Eugene Rosenberg working in his laboratory]]
Rosenberg's early work in Israel focused on myxobacteriology, hydrocarbon microbiology, surface-active polymers from [[Acinetobacter]], and [[bioremediation]]. In collaboration with his department colleagues [[Eliora Z. Ron]] and David Gutnik, he introduced the pioneering use of [[microorganisms]] and bioemulsifiers to treat oil pollution in oil tankers, pipelines and on beaches<ref name="Times"/>
In later years he collaborated with Yossi Loya (Zoology Dept., [[Tel Aviv University]]) to research coral disease. They demonstrated for the first time that [[coral bleaching]] is the result of an infectious disease<ref name="bleaching2"/> and that a rise in temperature due to global warming causes pathogenic micro organisms to be more active and cause infectious epidemic diseases.
His most recent work has developed the "[[hologenome]] concept of evolution" (together with Ilana Zilber-Rosenberg). This groundbreaking concept posits that the [[holobiont]] (host plus all of its associated microorganisms) and its hologenome (sum of the genetic information of the host and its symbiotic microorganisms), function as a unique biological entity and therefore as a level of selection in evolution. According to the hologenome concept microbial symbionts and the host interact in a cooperative way that affects the health of the holobiont within its environment, and the sum of these cooperative interactions characterizes the holobiont as a unique biological entity. He contends that under environmental stress, the [[microbiome]] can change more rapidly and in response to more processes than the host organism alone and thus influences the evolution of the holobiont. [[Prebiotic (nutrition)|Prebiotics]], [[probiotics]], [[synbiotics]] and [[phage therapy]] are conceived as applied aspects of the hologenome concept.

==Awards==
[[File:Eugene Liora.jpg|thumb|Eugene Rosenberg and Eliora Ron receiving the Prize for a "Beautiful Israel" at the President's residence]]
In 1983-1984 Rosenberg was appointed as a Fellow of the [[John Simon Guggenheim Memorial Foundation|Guggenheim Foundation]].<ref name = "guggenheim">{{Cite web |title= Guggenheim award announcements | url = http://www.gf.org/fellows/all-fellows/eugene-rosenberg/}}</ref> In 1992 he was awarded with the Pan Lab Prize of the Society of Industrial Microbiology<ref name="awards"/> and in 1993 he was awarded as a Fogarty International scholar at the National Institute Of Health ([[NIH]]).<ref name="asm"/> Jointly with [[Eliora Z. Ron|Prof.Eliora Ron]] he received the Israel Prize for a ''Beautiful Israel'' in 1995.<ref name = "beautiful israel">{{Cite web |title = The Council for A Beautiful Israel, Prize Awardees |url = http://www.israel-yafa.org.il/hebrew/Article.aspx?Item=765}}</ref> In 2002 he was awarded the Sakov Prize for ecological research and in 2003,<ref name="asm"/> the Procter & Gamble Prize of the [[American Society for Microbiology]] (ASM)<ref name="awards"/><ref name = asm_award>{{Cite web |title = American Society for Microbiology (ASM)|work = Awards Records |url = https://www.asm.org/index.php/membership/71-membership/archives/8313-ii-awards-awards-records}}</ref> and together with Yossi Loya, the Landau Prize by Mifaal Hapais in Life Sciences.<ref name = "landau">{{Cite web |title = Tel Aviv University Review of Events, Appointments and Honors, March, 2004|url = http://www2.tau.ac.il/news/engnews.asp?month=3&year=2004}}</ref>
In 2018 (with Ilana Zilber-Rosenberg), he received the Karl August Möbius Prize for lifetime achievements in symbiosis.<ref name="mobius"/>

Rosenberg has supervised numerous graduate students and postdocs. He is a Fellow of the [[American Academy of Microbiology]].<ref name="asm"/> He is also a Founding Member of the European Society of Microbiology.<ref name="tau_profile"/> He is the author of approximately 287 research papers and reviews, 15 books, and 16 patents.

==Selected publications==

# Rosenberg, E., ed. (1993). ''Microorganisms to Combat Pollution '' Springer. {{ISBN|978-94-010-4730-2}}

# Rosenberg, E. (1971). ''Cell and Molecular Biology: An Appreciation''  Holt, Rinehart and Winston, Inc., New York. {{ISBN|978-0030853128}}
